text: In this video, we'll be demonstrating laparoscopic myomectomy for a large fibroid without the use of vasopressin. In order to perform a surgery without vasopressin, typically what is done is bilateral uterine artery coagulation or ligation can be attempted.

The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament, carefully dissecting downwards parallel and medial to avoid the external iliac vessels. The uterine artery is identified crossing from lateral to medial, and after pushing the ureter down, the artery is coagulated.

The surgeon performs retroperitoneal dissection on the opposite side, revealing the uterine artery clearly visible at its origin as it crosses from lateral to medial. The artery is then safely coagulated.

The surgeon makes a large uterine incision at the most prominent part of the fibroid, which measures approximately fifteen to twenty centimeters. The fibroid is enucleated by extending the incision, revealing its deep extension into the submucosal plane. Care is taken to avoid damaging the endometrial cavity during removal, using traction and counter traction to extract the fibroid from the uterus.

The surgeon sutures the large uterine rent following uterine artery ligation, which has significantly reduced blood loss. The suturing technique focuses on approximating all dead spaces within the deep incision.

and hematoma formation in this cavity is absolutely avoided. Once the hematoma formation can be avoided, there occurs very minimal fibrosis and because of minimal fibrosis which occurs, the tissue respect is preserved and the tissue healing is much stronger which is mandatory when such a large fibroid is taken out and the lady wishes uterine conservation for future fertility.

One must also keep in mind that the uterine artery clipping which has to be performed does not affect the fertility in any way. However, it greatly reduces the chances of recurrence of these fibroids. Typically, in such a large case, otherwise vasopressin would have been used.

However, when we try and avoid vasopressin, the anesthetist is the happiest person because all the complications related to vasopressin which includes peripheral vasoconstriction, other cardiac complications can be absolutely avoided. in all these cases.

The surgeon performs multilayer suturing on the uterine incision, skillfully passing the needle at various angles and directions. Due to prior coagulation of the uterine arteries, there is minimal external blood loss visible during the suturing process.

The surgeon completes the final suturing using three to four layers of inverted baseball sutures on the uterine tissue. A Roders round knot is tied to secure the sutures and prevent slippage, ensuring strong scar formation for future fertility.

The surgeon removes the remaining portion of the large fibroid from the uterine cavity using a morcellation technique. The surgical site appears healthy with minimal blood loss at the end of the procedure.
